disclaimer: Analyst working dataset, not investment advice. Figures are estimates assembled from public reporting, company announcements, and regulatory filings as of the as-of date. Megawatts denote IT/critical load at full build unless noted; many campuses do not disclose exact MW, so values are order-of-magnitude. Each record carries a confidence flag (high / medium / low). Verify against primary sources before relying on any single number.
methodology: Scope: major AI-specific data-center campuses (frontier-training and large inference) plus the dedicated energy-supply assets contracted to power them. Not an exhaustive census of all US data centers (~hundreds of colo/enterprise facilities are excluded).
Megawatts: 'mw_it_full' = IT/critical load at full build. Total facility power (incl. cooling/overhead) is typically 1.2-1.4x higher (PUE).

Status vs. MW: 'status' reflects a campus's current PHASE (operational / under construction / announced / planned), but the MW shown is always FULL build. So 'under-construction GW' sums the full-build capacity of campuses in a construction phase and overstates MW physically energizing in any single year. External figures like '~5 GW under construction in 2026' count only near-term phased capacity and are not directly comparable.

Cross-checks (Jun 2026): nuclear figures reconcile exactly with public reporting (Crane/TMI 835 MW, Susquehanna 1,920 MW, Clinton 1,121 MW). Stargate Abilene ~1.2 GW / ~450k GB200, AWS Rainier ~2.2 GW / ~500k Trainium2, and GB200 economics (~$45k/GPU, ~1.7-2.0 kW IT/GPU) all match reported values. Many secondary campuses still cite homepage-level sources only — flagged low/medium confidence and the best candidates for /refresh-data.

Implied accelerators: where not directly reported, the dashboard estimates accelerator units from IT MW using a configurable ~2.0 kW per accelerator all-in (system-level, incl. CPU/networking/cooling-share). Calibrated so AWS Rainier ~2.2 GW => ~1.1M Trainium2 and xAI Colossus ~0.5 GW => ~230k GPUs. A rough planning figure, not a vendor count.
Silicon $: estimated from accelerator count x blended ASP (~$40-50k for GB-class systems, less for custom ASICs). Treat as directional TAM, not bookings.

Power gap: 'demand' = sum of campus IT MW by grid region; 'dedicated supply' = sum of power-asset MW linked to AI offtake in that region. A large gap implies reliance on existing grid headroom, merchant power, or not-yet-contracted generation.
Confidence: high = well-documented with disclosed figures; medium = reported but key numbers estimated; low = announced/early or coordinates and capacity approximate.
